name: Compression Fitting

aliases.name: Olive fitting
aliases.context: UK trade term — refers to the brass olive inside

aliases.name: Compression ring fitting
aliases.context: Descriptive term used in training materials

description: A mechanical fitting that joins two pipes or a pipe to a fixture using a compression nut, a brass olive (ring), and a fitting body. Tightening the nut compresses the olive against the pipe, creating a watertight seal without soldering.
useCases: Joining copper pipes in domestic plumbing
Connecting to valves and fixtures
Repairs where soldering is not possible (e.g., near timber or insulation)
installationNotes: Do not overtighten — typically 1 to 1.25 turns past hand-tight. Use PTFE tape or jointing compound on the olive for extra security. Always use pipe inserts when connecting to plastic pipe.

name: Push-Fit Fitting

aliases.name: Speedfit
aliases.context: JG Speedfit brand name — widely used generically in UK

aliases.name: SharkBite
aliases.context: Major US brand name — often used generically

description: A fitting that connects pipes by simply pushing the pipe into the fitting body. Internal grab ring teeth grip the pipe, while an O-ring creates the seal. No tools, solder, or compression nuts required. Can be removed and reused.
useCases: Fast repairs and joins without heat or tools
DIY plumbing projects
Tight spaces where a wrench can't reach
Temporary repairs on job sites
installationNotes: Ensure pipe is cut square and deburred. Push pipe in until it hits the internal stop — mark the insertion depth on the pipe first. Pipe inserts are required for plastic pipes. To disconnect, push the release collar toward the fitting and pull the pipe out.

name: Gate Valve

aliases.name: Full-bore valve
aliases.context: Describes the unrestricted flow when fully open

aliases.name: Wedge valve
aliases.context: Engineering term referring to internal gate shape

description: A valve that controls flow by raising or lowering an internal gate (wedge) via a handwheel. When fully open, provides unrestricted full-bore flow. Designed for fully open or fully closed positions — not for throttling or regulating flow.
useCases: Main water shut-off for a building
Isolating sections of a plumbing system
Municipal water supply lines
installationNotes: Gate valves should only be used fully open or fully closed. Using one partially open will cause the gate to erode and the valve to fail. Replace with a ball valve if throttling is needed. Operate the handwheel periodically to prevent seizing.

name: Ball Valve

aliases.name: Lever valve
aliases.context: Refers to the lever handle style

description: A valve with a hollow, perforated ball inside that pivots 90° to open or close flow. The lever handle makes operation fast — a quarter turn from fully open to fully closed. Offers reliable, tight shut-off and is widely used as the modern replacement for gate valves.
useCases: Isolating water supply to fixtures or appliances
Main shut-off valve
Gas line shut-off (yellow handle, gas-rated models only)
installationNotes: Red or blue handles indicate hot/cold water. Yellow handles indicate gas-rated valves — never use a water-rated ball valve on gas. Lever should be parallel to pipe when open, perpendicular when closed.

name: Stop Valve

aliases.name: Stopcock
aliases.context: UK term — often refers to the main stop valve

description: A valve that stops or starts flow using a washer pressed against a seat by a screw-down mechanism. The handwheel is turned multiple times to fully open or close. Often found under sinks and behind toilets for local fixture isolation.
useCases: Isolating individual fixtures (sinks, toilets, washing machines)
Main stopcock for a domestic property
Under-sink shut-off
installationNotes: The washer inside will wear over time and can be replaced without removing the valve body. Arrow on the body indicates flow direction — install accordingly. The main stopcock is typically located where the mains supply enters the building.

name: P-Trap

aliases.name: Waste trap
aliases.context: General term for any drain trap

aliases.name: Bottle trap
aliases.context: A compact variant of the same function — cylindrical body

aliases.name: U-bend
aliases.context: Older colloquial term (technically describes an S-trap)

description: A curved section of pipe beneath a drain that retains a small amount of water, creating a seal that prevents sewer gases from entering the building. The 'P' shape refers to the profile when viewed from the side. Every plumbed fixture must have a trap.
connectionType: Compression waste / Solvent weld / Push-fit waste
compatibility: Basin, sink, bath, and shower outlets
useCases: Under every sink, basin, bath, and shower
Connecting fixtures to the waste drainage system
Preventing sewer gas entry into occupied spaces
installationNotes: Ensure the trap is properly filled with water after installation — run the tap. If a fixture is unused for long periods, the water seal can evaporate (pour water in periodically). The trap depth (water seal) should be at least 50mm. Clean regularly to prevent blockages.

name: Float Valve

aliases.name: Ball cock
aliases.context: Traditional term — still widely used in UK trade

aliases.name: Ballvalve
aliases.context: Alternative spelling used in British standards

aliases.name: Toilet fill valve
aliases.context: US term for the cistern variant

description: A valve that automatically controls water level in a tank or cistern. A buoyant float rises with the water level and, through a lever arm or piston mechanism, shuts off the inlet when the desired level is reached. Found in toilet cisterns, cold water storage tanks, and header tanks.
useCases: Filling toilet cisterns to correct level
Maintaining water level in loft storage tanks
Feed and expansion tank for heating systems
installationNotes: Adjust the float arm or screw to set the correct water level — water should be about 25mm below the overflow. Part 1 valves are for high-pressure mains; Part 2 valves are for low-pressure tank-fed systems. Check the washer if the valve drips or doesn't shut off fully.

name: Check Valve
description: A valve that allows fluid to flow in one direction only. An internal flap, disc, or spring mechanism opens with forward flow and closes automatically if flow reverses, preventing backflow contamination. Required by water regulations at many connection points.
useCases: Preventing backflow from a heating system into mains water
At the outlet of booster pumps
Where water regulations require backflow protection (e.g., filling loops)
installationNotes: Install with the arrow on the body pointing in the direction of desired flow. Double-check valves are required where the risk category is higher. Must be testable and accessible for inspection in commercial installations.

name: Flexible Tap Connector

aliases.name: Braided hose
aliases.context: Describes the stainless steel braid exterior

description: A flexible, braided stainless steel hose with fittings on each end, used to connect water supply pipes to taps, toilets, and appliances. Eliminates the need for precise pipe alignment and simplifies installation in tight spaces.
useCases: Connecting taps to supply pipework
Toilet cistern water supply
Appliance connections in kitchens and utility rooms
installationNotes: Do not kink or twist the hose during installation — route gently. Check for leaks immediately after turning on supply. Replace every 5–10 years or immediately if any bulging, cracking, or corrosion is visible on the braid. Always use an isolating valve upstream.

name: Pipe Clip

aliases.name: Munsen ring
aliases.context: Heavy-duty split ring type for larger pipes

aliases.name: Saddle clip
aliases.context: Describes the shape of some metal clips

description: A bracket or clip used to secure pipes to walls, floors, joists, or other structural surfaces. Keeps pipework neat, supported, and prevents movement or stress on joints. Available in plastic snap-in, metal saddle, and heavy-duty split ring styles.
useCases: Securing exposed pipework to walls
Supporting horizontal pipe runs to prevent sagging
Fixing pipes in place before plastering or boxing in
installationNotes: Space clips at recommended intervals: 1.2m for horizontal 15mm copper, 1.8m for vertical. Use the correct size — a loose clip won't support the pipe. Allow for thermal expansion on long runs of plastic pipe (use slide clips, not fixed). Use plastic clips on plastic pipe to avoid damage.

name: Isolation Valve

aliases.name: Screwdriver valve
aliases.context: Describes the slotted head operated by a screwdriver

description: A small inline valve used to isolate the water supply to an individual fixture or appliance without shutting off the entire system. Operated by turning the slotted head 90° with a screwdriver or coin — no handwheel required.
useCases: Isolating a single tap for washer replacement
Shutting off water to a toilet for maintenance
Installed on each supply line to every fixture in a modern installation
installationNotes: Slot parallel to pipe = open. Slot perpendicular to pipe = closed. Building regulations now require isolation valves on the supply to every fixture. Compact size allows installation in tight spaces under basins and behind toilets.

name: Copper Elbow

aliases.name: Street elbow
aliases.context: Has one male and one female end — fits directly into another fitting

aliases.name: Sweep elbow
aliases.context: Long-radius elbow for gentler flow change

aliases.name: Solder ring elbow
aliases.context: Yorkshire fitting — pre-loaded solder ring inside

description: A fitting that changes the direction of a pipe run by 90° (or 45° in angled variants). Available as end-feed (requires solder), solder-ring (pre-loaded solder), or compression types. One of the most commonly used fittings in domestic plumbing.
useCases: Changing pipe direction around corners and obstacles
Routing pipe runs through walls and floors
Connecting vertical to horizontal runs
installationNotes: Clean pipe and fitting with wire wool before soldering. Apply flux evenly. For solder-ring fittings, heat evenly until a ring of solder appears around the joint. Let cool without disturbing. Compression elbows are used where heat is not safe (near timber, insulation).

name: Tee Fitting

aliases.name: Branch tee
aliases.context: Describes its function of creating a branch line

aliases.name: Equal tee
aliases.context: All three ports are the same size

aliases.name: Reducing tee
aliases.context: Branch port is smaller than the run ports

description: A T-shaped fitting that creates a branch connection from a main pipe run. Used to split or combine flow. Available as equal tees (all ports same size) or reducing tees (branch is a smaller diameter). Available in solder, compression, and push-fit types.
useCases: Branching a hot or cold supply to a new fixture
Creating distribution manifolds
Splitting flow for multiple radiators
installationNotes: When soldering, feed all three joints at once — heat the fitting body, not the pipe. On a live system, use a compression or push-fit tee to avoid draining down. Ensure flow direction is correct on reducing tees.

name: Pipe Reducer

aliases.name: Bell reducer
aliases.context: US term for the concentric shape

description: A fitting that connects two pipes of different diameters, allowing a transition between pipe sizes. Concentric reducers keep the centerline aligned; eccentric reducers offset the centerline (used to prevent air pockets in horizontal runs).
useCases: Transitioning from a 22mm main run to 15mm branch supply
Connecting older large-diameter pipework to modern smaller sizes
Reducing pipe size near terminal fixtures
installationNotes: Always reduce in the direction of flow where possible. Use eccentric reducers on horizontal runs to keep the bottom of the pipe flush and avoid air locks. Available in solder-ring and end-feed types.

name: Schrader Valve

aliases.name: Charging valve
aliases.context: Used when adding refrigerant to a system

aliases.name: Tire valve
aliases.context: Same valve mechanism used on car/bike tires

description: A spring-loaded valve core inside a threaded port, providing a sealed access point for connecting gauges and charging refrigerant into HVAC systems. The same mechanism used in tire valves. A cap must always be in place when not in use to prevent leaks.
useCases: Charging refrigerant into AC systems
Connecting manifold gauges for pressure readings
Recovering refrigerant during service
installationNotes: Always replace the cap after service — the cap is the primary seal, not the valve core. Use a Schrader core removal tool if the core needs replacement. Depressing the pin without a hose connected will release refrigerant (EPA violation).

name: Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)

aliases.name: Metering device
aliases.context: Functional category — TXV is one type of metering device

description: A precision metering device that regulates the flow of liquid refrigerant into the evaporator based on the superheat at the evaporator outlet. A sensing bulb attached to the suction line detects temperature changes and adjusts the valve opening to maintain optimal superheat.
useCases: Residential and commercial AC systems
Heat pump systems
Refrigeration units
installationNotes: Mount the sensing bulb at 10 or 2 o'clock on the suction line (never at the bottom — oil collects there). Insulate the bulb to prevent false readings from ambient air. Superheat adjustment screw: clockwise = more superheat (less refrigerant), counterclockwise = less superheat.

name: Filter Drier

aliases.name: Liquid line drier
aliases.context: Describes its location in the system

aliases.name: Moisture indicator
aliases.context: Some models include a sight glass with moisture indicator

description: An inline device on the liquid line that removes moisture and particulate contaminants from the refrigerant. Contains a desiccant core (molecular sieve) that absorbs water, and a filter screen that traps debris. Protects the TXV and compressor from damage.
sizes: ½" OD (matched to liquid line size)
useCases: Installed on every AC and refrigeration system liquid line
Replaced after any compressor burnout (critical — contaminated oil)
Replaced after system has been opened to atmosphere
installationNotes: Arrow on the body indicates flow direction — install with flow from condenser toward TXV. Replace every time the system is opened for major repair. Overheating during brazing can damage the desiccant core — use a wet rag as a heat sink. Always pull a deep vacuum after replacement.

name: Air Handler Unit

aliases.name: Indoor unit
aliases.context: In split system context — the part inside the building

aliases.name: Blower unit
aliases.context: Colloquial term focusing on the fan component

description: The indoor component of a split HVAC system containing the blower fan, evaporator coil, air filter, and sometimes supplemental electric heat strips. Draws return air from the building, passes it over the coil (for cooling or heating), and distributes conditioned air through the ductwork.
compatibility: Matched outdoor condensing unit (same brand/AHRI rating)
useCases: Central residential HVAC systems
Commercial buildings
Paired with heat pump or AC condensing unit
installationNotes: Must be matched to the outdoor unit by AHRI certification for rated efficiency. Condensate drain must be properly trapped and routed. Filter should be accessible for regular replacement. Install on a drain pan in attic locations to catch condensate overflow.

name: Damper

aliases.name: Fire damper
aliases.context: Safety type — closes automatically in a fire

aliases.name: VAV box
aliases.context: Variable Air Volume — motorized damper with controller

description: A device installed inside ductwork that regulates or blocks airflow. Manual dampers use a lever to adjust a blade; motorized dampers are controlled by thermostats or building automation systems. Fire dampers are safety devices that close automatically when heat-activated.
useCases: Balancing airflow between rooms
Zone control in multi-zone systems
Fire safety in commercial ductwork penetrations
installationNotes: Manual dampers: lever parallel to duct = open, perpendicular = closed. Fire dampers must be accessible for inspection — install access doors. Motorized dampers need 24V power and control signal from thermostat or BAS. Label all dampers clearly for future technicians.

name: Condenser Fan Motor

aliases.name: Universal condenser motor
aliases.context: Aftermarket replacement that fits multiple units

description: The electric motor that drives the fan blade on top of (or beside) the outdoor condensing unit. Draws ambient air across the condenser coil to reject heat from the refrigerant. Failure causes high head pressure and system shutdown on safety.
compatibility: Specific to condensing unit model (check OEM part number)
Universal motors available for common HP/RPM/rotation specs
useCases: Replacement when motor fails (seized bearings, burned windings)
Upgrade to more efficient motor
Emergency repair to restore cooling
installationNotes: Match HP, RPM, rotation direction, shaft diameter, and voltage to original motor. Wire the run capacitor correctly — wrong capacitance will damage the motor. Note rotation direction before removing old motor. Disconnect power at the breaker before starting work.

name: Expansion Vessel
description: A sealed vessel with an internal rubber diaphragm that absorbs the expansion of water as it heats in a sealed heating system. One side contains pre-charged air/nitrogen; the other side connects to the system water. Prevents dangerous pressure buildup and frequent pressure relief valve discharge.
useCases: Every sealed heating system requires one
Replacement when pressure drops repeatedly (failed diaphragm)
Additional vessel when system has been extended
installationNotes: Pre-charge pressure must match the system cold fill pressure (typically 1.0–1.5 bar). Check and adjust with a dry tire gauge on the Schrader valve before filling the system. If the vessel is waterlogged (water comes from the Schrader valve), the diaphragm has failed — replace the vessel.

name: Circulator Pump

aliases.name: Zone pump
aliases.context: When used for a single zone

aliases.name: Wet rotor pump
aliases.context: Describes the motor design — rotor runs in the system water

aliases.name: Grundfos pump
aliases.context: Major brand name — often used generically (like 'Hoover' for vacuum)

description: An electrically powered pump that circulates hot water through a central heating system (from boiler through radiators/underfloor heating and back). Wet rotor design means the motor is cooled and lubricated by the system water flowing through it.
useCases: Pumping hot water through radiator circuits
Zoned heating systems (one pump per zone)
Underfloor heating distribution
installationNotes: Install with the motor shaft horizontal (not vertical) to prevent bearing wear. Bleed air from the pump via the bleed screw after filling. Modern pumps (e.g., Grundfos UPS2, UPS3) auto-detect speed — older pumps have manual speed settings (1, 2, 3). Isolator valves on both sides for easy replacement.

name: Pressure Relief Valve

aliases.name: PRV
aliases.context: Common abbreviation (can also mean Pressure Reducing Valve — context matters)

aliases.name: Safety valve
aliases.context: Functional term emphasizing its safety role

aliases.name: T&P valve
aliases.context: Temperature & Pressure relief valve — US term for water heater version

description: A spring-loaded safety valve that opens automatically when system pressure exceeds a set limit (typically 3 bar for domestic boilers), releasing water to prevent dangerous over-pressurization. The discharge must be piped to a safe, visible location. This is a critical safety device.
compatibility: Unvented hot water cylinders (set at 6 or 7 bar)
useCases: Mandatory safety device on every sealed heating system
Protects against dangerous pressure buildup
Discharge indicates a system fault (failed expansion vessel, overfilling)
installationNotes: NEVER cap, plug, or block the discharge from a PRV — it is a life-safety device. Discharge pipe must fall continuously to a visible, safe location outside the building. If the PRV is frequently discharging, investigate the cause: expansion vessel failure, filling loop left open, or system overfilled. Replace the PRV — do not attempt to repair it.

name: Zone Valve

aliases.name: Motorized valve
aliases.context: General term for any motor-driven valve

aliases.name: Mid-position valve
aliases.context: UK term — 3-port valve that can serve heating, hot water, or both

aliases.name: Honeywell valve
aliases.context: Dominant brand — V4043H (2-port) and V4073A (3-port) are industry standard

description: A motorized valve that controls the flow of hot water to different zones in a central heating system (e.g., radiators vs. hot water cylinder). Controlled by the programmer/timer and room thermostat. The motor opens the valve; a spring returns it to closed.
useCases: Directing heating to radiators when room thermostat calls for heat
Directing hot water to the cylinder when cylinder thermostat calls
S-Plan: separate 2-port valve for each zone
Y-Plan: single 3-port mid-position valve
installationNotes: The motor head can be replaced without draining the system — unclip and swap. When the valve reaches fully open, a microswitch fires to tell the boiler to ignite. Check the microswitch if the boiler won't fire. Manual lever on top allows opening for filling/draining without power.

name: Heat Exchanger

aliases.name: Plate heat exchanger
aliases.context: Most common type in domestic boilers

aliases.name: Brazed plate
aliases.context: Describes construction — stainless steel plates brazed together

aliases.name: Primary heat exchanger
aliases.context: The main burner-to-water exchanger in a boiler

aliases.name: Secondary heat exchanger
aliases.context: In combi boilers — heats DHW from primary circuit

description: A device that transfers heat between two fluids without mixing them. In boilers, the primary heat exchanger transfers heat from the gas burner flame to the heating water. In combi boilers, a secondary plate heat exchanger heats domestic hot water from the primary heating water.
sizes: Replacement must match boiler model exactly
compatibility: Specific to boiler make and model
useCases: Core component of every boiler — transfers combustion heat to water
Secondary DHW heating in combi boilers
External plate heat exchangers for system separation
installationNotes: Scaling and sludge are the main enemies — a clean system with inhibitor protects the heat exchanger. Kettling noise (rumbling/banging) usually indicates scale buildup on the heat exchanger. Flushing the system with a powerflush machine can restore performance. Replacement is model-specific and often requires significant boiler disassembly.

name: Lockshield Valve

aliases.name: Balancing valve
aliases.context: Functional term — used to balance the system

description: A valve on one end of a radiator (opposite the TRV or manual valve) used to balance the heating system. Has a protective cap that must be removed to access the adjusting spindle. Set during commissioning to ensure each radiator heats at the correct rate relative to others.
useCases: Balancing a central heating system so all radiators heat evenly
Partially closing to restrict flow to radiators nearest the pump
Draining a radiator (close the other valve, open the lockshield with a bucket)
installationNotes: Balancing procedure: open all lockshields fully, then progressively close those on radiators nearest the pump. Measure the temperature difference between flow and return at each radiator — aim for 11–12°C difference across each radiator. Use an adjustable spanner or lockshield key to turn the spindle.

name: Thermostatic Radiator Valve (TRV)

aliases.name: Room thermostat valve
aliases.context: Describes its role in room temperature control

description: A self-regulating valve fitted to a radiator that controls room temperature by adjusting water flow based on the surrounding air temperature. A wax or liquid-filled sensor in the valve head expands and contracts to open or close the valve pin — no electrical power needed.
compatibility: Standard M30 × 1.5 head connection (most European brands)
useCases: Individual room temperature control
Energy saving — reduces flow to radiators in warm rooms
Required by building regulations on most radiators in new installations
installationNotes: Do NOT fit a TRV in the same room as the room thermostat — they will conflict. Fit on the flow side of the radiator (lockshield on the return). Keep the TRV head away from heat sources and curtains that could give false readings. Smart TRV heads are available that replace the mechanical head with app-controlled versions.

name: Filling Loop

aliases.name: Bypass link
aliases.context: Sometimes confused — a bypass is different

aliases.name: Keyless filling loop
aliases.context: Modern type that doesn't require a key/lever

description: A short braided hose or pipe assembly that temporarily connects the mains cold water supply to the sealed heating system for filling and pressurizing. Includes one or two isolation valves and often a check valve to prevent heating water contaminating the mains supply.
compatibility: Combi boilers (many have a built-in filling loop)
System boilers with external filling loop
useCases: Initial fill and pressurization of a new heating system
Topping up pressure when the gauge drops below 1 bar
Refilling after draining for maintenance
installationNotes: Water regulations require the filling loop to be DISCONNECTABLE — do not permanently connect the mains to a heating system. Fill to 1.0–1.5 bar cold (check boiler manual). Close both valves AND disconnect the hose after filling. If pressure drops frequently, check for leaks or a failed expansion vessel.
